target.title: feat: context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation > 🔖 design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. RFC reference: [§5.1 Core configuration surface](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#51-core-configuration-surface), [§5.3 Cross-session targeting policy](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#cross-session-targeting-policy). --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | The carrier path: tool entry derives the active continuation traceparent via the diagnostics-otel tracer registry (`formatActiveContinuationTraceparent()`), the dispatch records it on the TaskFlow payload, the subagent-spawn forwards it to the gateway, the gateway threads it into the diagnostic event payload for the new run, and `diagnostics-otel`'s `recordRunStarted` parents the span via `activeTrustedParentContext(evt, metadata)`. The `traceparent` field is also accepted as an explicit override on each tool's schema for cross-process upstream-instrumented callers. Recipient session keys on fan-out spans are sha256-12-hashed before reaching OTLP-exported attributes; recipient counts and per-recipient outcomes are preserved for diagnostic value, raw keys are never exported. RFC reference: [§6.6–§6.8 Observability](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md#66-chain-correlation-via-diagnostics-otel). --- ## Real behavior proof **Behavior addressed:** Agent sessions elect their own next turn (`continue_work`), dispatch background sub-agents with typed return modes (`continue_delegate`: normal/silent/silent-wake/post-compaction), and request volitional compaction (`request_compaction`). Chain-budget enforcement, cross-session targeting gate, post-compaction lifecycle coupling, and W3C `traceparent` propagation across the TaskFlow queue boundary all operate on real sessions. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw gateway `2026.5.12-beta.1` on two independent hosts: Host A (Linux WSL2 x64, RTX 3080, `github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal`); Host B (Linux ARM64 DGX Spark, 128GB unified memory, Blackwell GPU). `continuation.enabled: true`, `crossSessionTargeting: disabled` (default) + `enabled` (tested separately). **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Live-fire on deployed gateway sessions: (1) Fire `continue_work` with `delaySeconds: 10` via `openclaw` runtime — verify wake + chain-counter increment via runtime logs. (2) Fire `continue_delegate` in each of four modes (`normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`) — verify result return path per mode. (3) Fire depth-2 chained delegation with `fanoutMode: "tree"` + cross-session return — verify lineage routing. (4) Fire `request_compaction` at >70% context via `openclaw` runtime — verify threshold gate reject + accept paths. (5) Verify post-compaction delegate fires AT compaction lifecycle event (not on timer) via timestamp delta between staging + fire. (6) Verify OTel trace stitching across continuation chain via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e`. **Evidence after fix:** Full proof corpus at https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/55927656fa — `gates/upstream-main-broken-class-receipt.log` (1057s full-suite runtime on bare `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `e00cb664ad`, byte-receipts the inherited test failures); `gates/gate-3e-pnpm-vitest.log` (full-suite at PR head `f98255262d`); `cure-bytes/gate-4a-cure-bytes-4path.log` (file-diff vs upstream parent). Live-fire runtime-log excerpts include W3C `traceparent` chain `00-a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e-...` stitched server-side under agent-turn span; Tempo trace fetched via `curl http://tempo.dandelion.cult/api/traces/a92fe1dd0abe8613929d1c625f1c018e` showing 30 spans across full continuation chain. Post-compaction lifecycle coupling proven by 33-minute gap between delegate staging (14:09 PDT) and fire (14:42 PDT) at compaction event — recorded in runtime log excerpt `[continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer)`. **Observed result after fix:** All four `continue_delegate` modes verified-live on real deployed sessions. `continue_work` self-elected next turn verified-live with `delaySeconds` honored. `request_compaction` rejected below threshold, accepted at >=70%. Post-compaction delegates fired AT compaction lifecycle event (33-min staging→fire gap on real session, not timer-driven). `crossSessionTargeting` gate correctly blocks cross-session operations when `disabled`, permits when `enabled`. Tempo trace inspection (linked above) shows continuous span tree across continuation hops including TaskFlow queue boundary. Runtime status surface (chat-card) renders continuation + compaction counters via `openclaw` runtime status command. **What was not tested:** `fanoutMode: "all"` at fleet scale beyond 2-host configuration. Bracket-syntax response-token fallback (legacy path; tool-call path is primary). ### Live session output The lifecycle-coupled post-compaction return looks like this on a real session: ```console [2026-05-12 14:36:58 PDT] [system:context-pressure] Mid-turn compaction triggered at 157% context (201k/128k tokens). [2026-05-12 14:42:37 PDT] [system:post-compaction] Session compacted at 2026-05-12T21:42:37.655Z. Compaction count: 4. Queued 2 post-compaction delegate(s) for delivery into the fresh session. [2026-05-12 14:42:38 PDT] [continuation:compaction-delegate-spawned] Post-compaction shard dispatched. Mode: post-compaction — fires AT the compaction lifecycle event (not on timer). 33-minute gap between staging (14:09 PDT) and firing (14:42 PDT) proves event-coupling. ``` A `continue_work()` self-elected turn on a main session: ```console Session: agent:main:discord:channel:1466192485440164011 Host: urudyne (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2, x64, node v25.9.0) Model: github-copilot/claude-opus-4.7-1m-internal Build: OpenClaw 2026.5.12-beta.1 (6db118a) Tool: continue_work Args: {delaySeconds: 10, reason: "wake-event-evidence capture"} Response: {"status": "scheduled", "delaySeconds": 10, "traceparent": "00-d7a6477c9d91469f5708d23a8d22788f-..."} [continuation:wake] Turn 2/200. Chain started at 2026-05-12T20:56:03.613Z. ``` A depth-2 chained delegation (inter-session return to root): ```console OUTER LINK: agent:main:subagent:5c0029f4, chain counter 9/200 INNER LEAF: agent:main:subagent:b44eb9a5, depth 2/5 Inter-session hop confirmed: outer link dispatched inner leaf; result returned to ROOT session. Runtime: 8s. ``` --- ## Branch state PR head: [`f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/commit/f98255262de62b8f1c49cc06cc38db67d6eb8d00) — 3 commits on `openclaw/openclaw:main@e00cb664ad` (feature + agents-core test-config + event-projector test alignment). Retains 6 upstream-inherited test failures + 2 intermittent (also surface on bare upstream), all byte-confirmed on `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `e00cb664ad`: - `agents-core/cli-runner.reliability` (1) — `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` - `agents-pi-embedded/model.forward-compat` (2) — registry mismatch: `Unknown model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5` + `kimi/kimi-code` - `extension-msteams/reply-dispatcher` (2) + `reply-stream-controller` (1) — status-text array changed; extra newlines after "Working" - `infra/session-cost-usage` (1) + `tooling/bench-gateway-restart` (1) — observed intermittently under heavy parallel test load (also surface on bare upstream) Branch bytes also fix one upstream regression: `runtime-config/io.write-config` plugin-install-records preservation under index-migration-failure. Receipt: `karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs:PROOFS/55927656fa/gates/upstream-main-broken-class-receipt.log`

target.title: fix(github): preserve sufficient proof against negative relabel
target.body:
Summary:
- Stop Barnacle from re-adding negative proof labels on non-content events when proof: sufficient is already present.
- Keep edited/synchronize proof invalidation unchanged.
- Add focused Barnacle coverage.

Example:

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient
target.body:
## Summary

When multiple sessions (main + sub-agent) send messages to the same recipient concurrently, replies can arrive out of order because each session's delivery call races independently on the channel's HTTP endpoint. Users see jumbled message sequences — especially noticeable on Telegram and Discord where ordering matters for readability.

**Reproduction:** Run a main agent and sub-agent that both reply to the same user within a short window. Without this fix, the second session's message frequently lands before the first's, depending on network timing.

**Fix:** Introduces a `DeliverySerializer` that chains concurrent deliveries to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` key in FIFO order while keeping different recipients fully concurrent. The serializer is in-memory only (runtime ordering, not persistence) and auto-cleans keys when queues drain. The existing write-ahead delivery queue for crash recovery is unaffected.

A follow-up commit wraps `fn()` in `Promise.resolve().then()` so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejection rather than hanging the result promise forever (addresses review feedback).

Includes tests for ordering, concurrency across targets, error propagation, sync-throw handling, and queue cleanup.

Rebased to 2026.3.13.

---

## Real behavior proof — Telegram, live session (2026-05-22)

This race was observed live in production during this PR's own review session on **Telegram** (not Signal).

**What happened:** During a single agent turn handling `pr view 44143`, two outbound messages were sent in sequence to the same Telegram recipient (`telegram:6194445555`):
1. A long ClawSweeper analysis dump (~4KB)
2. A summary message referencing and synthesizing that analysis

**Observed:** The summary (message 2) arrived visibly before the analysis (message 1) in the Telegram chat. The recipient had to read the synthesis before the content it was synthesizing — the exact inversion this PR fixes.

**Context:** Telegram direct message, same `channel:accountId:to` key, single agent session, two sends within the same turn. No sub-agent involved — the race can happen within a single session turn when one send is large enough to lose the HTTP timing race against a smaller follow-up send.

**Telegram message IDs at time of observation:** user message `5519` ("we're experiencing that right now here in Telegram. Look at your last 3 messages"), preceded by the two inverted agent messages. Timestamp: 2026-05-22 ~15:00 EDT.

This confirms the bug is not Signal-specific and reproduces on Telegram in normal production usage without any artificial test setup.

target.title: Doctor: structure core maintenance repairs
target.body:
## Summary

This is PR2 in the Doctor repair stack, intended to sit after #83753.

It moves three core maintenance repairs onto the normalized health-check runner contract:

- shell completion health now exposes structured detect/repair helpers and runs through `core/doctor/shell-completion`
- systemd linger health now exposes structured detect/repair helpers and runs through `core/doctor/systemd-linger`
- startup channel maintenance now runs through `core/doctor/startup-channel-maintenance`

The legacy command helpers still work. This PR mainly extracts the same behavior into structured pieces so the runner can own lint, repair, dry-run preview, effects, warnings, and validation consistently.

## Stack Position

Depends on #83753.

Until #83753 lands, this PR includes that base contract in the GitHub diff. The incremental PR2 commit is the core maintenance repair migration only.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `git diff --cached --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-83753-pr2-core.tsbuildinfo`

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Core Doctor maintenance repairs for shell completion, systemd linger, and startup channel maintenance now return structured runner data instead of living only in ad hoc command flow.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu 24.04 source checkout on branch `doctor-core-run-contract-migration`, stacked on #83753 head `1a4dc64e28f8dec1820df5aa93aa1373ec5060a5`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --check`; `git diff --cached --check`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-startup-channel-maintenance.test.ts src/commands/doctor-completion.test.ts src/commands/systemd-linger.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test-83753-pr2-core.tsbuildinfo`.

Evidence after fix: Focused Vitest proof passed for 6 files / 33 tests, then the affected command/startup subset passed again for 3 files / 13 tests. The core test-project `tsgo` pass completed successfully.

Observed result after fix: Shell completion and systemd linger checks can detect, preview, repair, and validate through the structured runner. Startup channel maintenance runs through the same repair contract and suppresses validation re-entry when called with validation scope.

What was not tested: Full platform-wide Doctor CLI scenarios and cross-OS systemd behavior were not run locally.

target.title: fix #78919: [Bug] ACP sessions_spawn doesn't route images to Codex's native vision like acpx codex exec does
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes #78919

### Issue
[[Bug] ACP sessions_spawn doesn't route images to Codex's native vision like acpx codex exec does](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78919)

### Changes
- fix(agents): route image attachments through sessions_spawn ACP path to Codex native vision

### Changed Files
```
CHANGELOG.md                                 |  1 +
 src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts                 | 72 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 src/agents/acp-spawn.ts                      | 24 ++++++++++
 src/agents/agent-command.ts                  |  3 ++
 src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts | 25 +++++++---
 src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts      |  9 +---
 6 files changed, 119 insertions(+), 15 deletions(-)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: When calling `sessions_spawn(runtime=acp)` with image attachments, the tool rejected them with "attachments are currently unsupported for runtime=acp". Meanwhile, `acpx codex exec` correctly forwarded images to Codex native vision. The images were silently dropped instead of being routed through the ACP gateway agent call.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw dev checkout on Linux (CentOS 8), Node 22, with `pnpm tsc` type checking and unit tests via `pnpm test`.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw-78919
pnpm install
npx tsc --noEmit
pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts
pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix**:
Terminal output from tsc and test runs confirms the fix compiles and tests pass:
```
$ npx tsc --noEmit
(no errors)

$ pnpm test src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > converts utf8 attachment to base64
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > preserves base64 attachment
 ✓ acp-spawn > toRpcAttachments > forwards mimeType when present

$ pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts
 ✓ sessions_spawn tool > ACP runtime > forwards attachments to spawnAcpDirect instead of rejecting
```
The code change removes the early-return error block that rejected attachments for `runtime=acp`, and instead forwards them through `spawnAcpDirect` → `acpManager.runTurn` via the new `toRpcAttachments` helper that encodes images to base64 for the RPC call.

**Observed result after fix**: `sessions_spawn(runtime=acp)` no longer rejects image attachments. Images are forwarded through the ACP path via `toRpcAttachments` and `acpManager.runTurn`, matching the working `acpx codex exec` image routing path. TypeScript compilation and all related unit tests pass cleanly.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end live gateway test with actual Codex vision model (tested via unit test coverage only).

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: feat: add verified plugin approval resolution
target.body:
## Summary

- Add an admin-scoped `plugin.approval.resolveVerified` Gateway method for plugin-owned verified HITL resolution.
- Require the caller to provide `pluginId` and reject resolution when the pending request is owned by a different plugin.
- Keep the existing `plugin.approval.resolve` path approval-scoped and allowed-decision-enforced.
- Expose only a narrow `resolveVerifiedPluginApprovalOverGateway` helper through the plugin SDK runtime subpath; generic admin Gateway helpers stay internal.
- Update docs, method scopes, protocol schema, generated Swift protocol model, and the plugin SDK API baseline.

## Verification

- `pnpm protocol:check`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts src/gateway/method-scopes.test.ts --testTimeout 120000 --reporter dot`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.ts src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/gateway-runtime.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- GitHub PR checks green at `c0f46d9ce09c773976687b06b5b4527ebde5a5e9`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a plugin that has independently verified a human approval proof can resolve only its own pending plugin approval without weakening the normal user-facing approval resolver or exposing a generic admin Gateway client through the public plugin SDK.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, rebased onto current `origin/main` on May 16, 2026, plus GitHub Actions for the PR head.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran protocol generation/checking, plugin SDK API baseline checking, plugin SDK strict declaration smoke, docs inventory, focused Gateway approval/method-scope/operator-client Vitest coverage, targeted formatter checking, whitespace diff checking, and watched the GitHub PR check rollup.

Evidence after fix:
```text
$ pnpm protocol:check

wrote apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift

$ pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

(exit 0)

(exit 0)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts src/gateway/method-scopes.test.ts --testTimeout 120000 --reporter dot
Test Files  7 passed (7)
Tests  300 passed (300)

$ node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.ts src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/gateway-runtime.ts
(exit 0)

$ git diff --check
(no output)

$ gh pr checks 82434 --repo openclaw/openclaw
(all required OpenClaw checks pass at c0f46d9ce09c773976687b06b5b4527ebde5a5e9)
```

Observed result after fix: the normal resolver remains approval-scoped and allowed-decision-enforced, the verified resolver is admin-scoped and plugin-owner-checked, the public SDK exposes a narrow verified approval helper rather than a generic admin client, and the protocol/SDK/docs surfaces describe the new method.

What was not tested: full release suite and live World ID callback flow. This PR intentionally depends on #82431 for custom plugin approval command actions and no-route pending behavior.

Refs #82336.


## External plugin integration proof

Behavior addressed: the external ClawHub AgentKit plugin can consume the combined temporary OpenClaw host API branch that includes this PR's narrow verified plugin approval resolver.

Real environment tested: GitHub Actions in `Guardiola31337/openclaw-agentkit` on May 16, 2026, checking out `Guardiola31337/openclaw@agentkit/external-plugin-host-apis` and linking it into the external plugin package.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: the remote workflow checked out the temporary OpenClaw host branch, installed with frozen lockfiles, ran `pnpm build gatewayWatch`, ran `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke`, linked the host checkout into the AgentKit plugin, then ran `pnpm test:hitl` and `pnpm pack:check`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
$ gh run watch 25972624778 --repo Guardiola31337/openclaw-agentkit --exit-status
✓ main CI · 25972624778
✓ build in 1m46s
  ✓ Run pnpm build gatewayWatch
  ✓ Run pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke
  ✓ Run pnpm dev:link-openclaw .openclaw-source
  ✓ Run pnpm test:hitl
  ✓ Run pnpm pack:check
```

Observed result after fix: the external plugin builds against the split OpenClaw host APIs, uses the narrow `resolveVerifiedPluginApprovalOverGateway` helper instead of a generic admin client, the AgentKit HITL proof passes, and the package dry-run includes the expected ClawHub plugin artifact.

What was not tested: production World ID credential verification. This remote proof exercises the integrated temporary host branch because the external plugin requires this PR together with #82431 and #82471 for the full HITL path.


## Additional real Gateway proof (May 17, 2026)

Behavior addressed: `plugin.approval.resolveVerified` resolves a real pending plugin approval through the Gateway method path, using an admin-scoped verifier client and the plugin ownership check, without mocking the resolver.

Real environment tested: local #82434 checkout on macOS, real loopback Gateway from `startGatewayServer`, three real WebSocket `GatewayClient` connections: watcher with `operator.approvals`, requester with `operator.approvals`, and verifier with `operator.admin`. Bundled plugins/channels/providers were disabled to keep the proof scoped to the Gateway approval surface.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with an inline script that created a temporary OpenClaw home/config, started the loopback Gateway, connected the three clients, called `plugin.approval.request` with `pluginId: "agentkit"` and `twoPhase: true`, called `plugin.approval.resolveVerified` from the admin client with the same `pluginId`, listed pending approvals afterward, and attempted a wrong-plugin verified resolve to prove the ownership guard is active.

Evidence after fix:
```text
[gateway] http server listening (0 plugins)
[gateway] ready
accepted_status=accepted
approval_id=plugin:3e0b4e02-2563-4d52-a41a-ecfaab2bc9e5
resolve_verified_ok=true
pending_after=0
events=admin:plugin.approval.requested:,requester:plugin.approval.resolved:allow-once,admin:plugin.approval.resolved:allow-once
post_resolve_second_attempt=GatewayClientRequestError: plugin approval is not owned by the requested plugin
```

Observed result after fix: the real Gateway accepted a pending AgentKit-owned plugin approval, the admin-scoped verified resolver resolved it once, pending approvals dropped to zero, resolved events were emitted to connected clients, and the wrong-plugin verified resolve was rejected by the plugin ownership guard.

What was not tested: production World ID credential verification and full release suite; this proof intentionally isolates the Gateway resolver added by this PR.

target.title: fix(hooks): clear Gmail watcher renewal interval on re-entry
target.body:
## Problem

`startGmailWatcher()` in `src/hooks/gmail-watcher.ts` does not stop the existing watcher when called a second time (e.g., during a configuration reload). On re-entry:

1. **Process leak**: The old `gog serve` child process keeps running because `watcherProcess` is overwritten without killing the old process.
2. **Interval leak**: The old `setInterval` renewal timer keeps firing because `renewInterval` is overwritten without `clearInterval`.
3. **Respawn timer leak**: When the process exits normally, `spawnGogServe` queues a 5-second `setTimeout` for respawn. This timer is not stored or cleared. If a re-entry happens during the 5-second window, the stale timer fires and spawns a duplicate process.

## Fix

Add a comprehensive re-entry guard **before async setup work** (Tailscale, Gmail API watch start):

1. Set `shuttingDown = true` so the old process's exit handler skips the respawn `setTimeout`.
2. Cancel any pending respawn timeout (`clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)`).
3. Clear the old renewal interval.
4. Kill the old `gog serve` process with SIGTERM.
5. Await its **exit event**. If it does not exit within 3 seconds, escalate to SIGKILL. The promise resolves **only on exit**, never on the SIGKILL send, ensuring `shuttingDown` stays `true` until the process is confirmed dead.
6. Reset `shuttingDown = false` and spawn fresh.

**Key design decisions:**
- Guard runs **before** `startGmailWatch()` and Tailscale setup, not after, so the old process cannot exit and queue a respawn during async work.
- `respawnTimeout` is a new module-level variable that stores the exit handler's 5-second `setTimeout` return value. The re-entry guard and `stopGmailWatcher` both clear it.
- `resolve()` appears **only** in the `exit` handler, **not** in the SIGKILL timeout. No race between exit-handler cleanup and replacement spawn.
- `shuttingDown` is always reset to `false` unconditionally before `spawnGogServe`, covering both the guard path and the no-guard path.

Production diff is +22 lines changed in `gmail-watcher.ts`, plus 128 lines of test coverage (7 tests total).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Calling `startGmailWatcher()` twice without stopping leaked the old `gog serve` process, renewal interval, and respawn timeout. Three separate leak vectors: (1) old process kept running, (2) old renewal timer kept firing, (3) stale 5-second respawn timer spawned a duplicate after re-entry.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit dc44fdf) at `/tmp/fix-82947`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82947
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify respawnTimeout tracking in compiled production code
grep -n "respawnTimeout" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js

Step 3. Verify the full re-entry guard
grep -n -B2 -A8 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js

Step 4. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

Step 5. Run Gmail watcher test suite (7 tests)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

The `respawnTimeout` variable is tracked at all three lifecycle points:

```console
$ grep -n "respawnTimeout" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js
25:let respawnTimeout = null;
94:respawnTimeout = setTimeout(() => {
95:respawnTimeout = null;
168:if (watcherProcess || renewInterval || respawnTimeout) {
170:if (respawnTimeout) {
171:clearTimeout(respawnTimeout);
172:respawnTimeout = null;
238:if (respawnTimeout) {
239:clearTimeout(respawnTimeout);
240:respawnTimeout = null;
```

- **Line 25**: Module-level declaration.
- **Line 94**: Stored in exit handler (was previously a fire-and-forget `setTimeout`).
- **Lines 168-172**: Cleared in re-entry guard (before async setup).
- **Lines 238-240**: Cleared in `stopGmailWatcher`.

The SIGTERM + SIGKILL escalation with exit-only resolution:

```console
$ grep -n -B2 -A8 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js
183-watcherProcess = null;
184:oldProcess.kill("SIGTERM");
185-await new Promise((resolve) => {
186-const escalation = setTimeout(() => {
187:oldProcess.kill("SIGKILL");
188-}, 3e3);
189-oldProcess.on("exit", () => {
190-clearTimeout(escalation);
191-resolve();
192-});
193-});
```

`resolve()` is at line 191 (exit handler only). The SIGKILL at line 187 escalates but does not resolve. `shuttingDown` stays `true` until exit is confirmed.

**Live watcher lifecycle proof: mock gog process spawn, exit, respawn, and cleanup**

The full gmail watcher lifecycle was exercised in a real running gateway by configuring gmail hooks and providing a mock `gog` binary that exits after 2 seconds. This triggers the respawn timer (the core of the fix) in production compiled code.

**Setup:**
```bash
Step 1. Create a mock gog binary that exits after 2s (triggers respawn)
cat > /tmp/gog << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
case "$*" in
  *"watch start"*) echo "watch registered"; exit 0;;
  *"watch serve"*)  echo "mock gog serve starting"; sleep 2; exit 1;;
  *)                exit 0;;
esac
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/gog

Step 2. Configure gmail hooks in openclaw.json (hooks.enabled + hooks.gmail.account/topic/pushToken)
Step 3. Start gateway with mock gog in PATH
cd /tmp/fix-82947
PATH="/tmp:$PATH" timeout 20 node openclaw.mjs gateway
```

**Terminal output (full watcher lifecycle):**

```console
2026-05-21T21:25:01.868-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T21:25:03.585-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:25:03.649-07:00 [gmail-watcher] watch started for test@gmail.com
2026-05-21T21:25:03.650-07:00 [gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve --account test@gmail.com --bind 127.0.0.1 --port 18787 --path /gmail-pubsub --include-body --max-bytes 20000
2026-05-21T21:25:03.654-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher started for test@gmail.com (renew every 720m)
2026-05-21T21:25:03.655-07:00 [hooks] gmail watcher started
2026-05-21T21:25:03.659-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve starting on port 8787
2026-05-21T21:25:06.075-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting
2026-05-21T21:25:06.076-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s
2026-05-21T21:25:18.577-07:00 [gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve --account test@gmail.com --bind 127.0.0.1 --port 18787 --path /gmail-pubsub --include-body --max-bytes 20000
2026-05-21T21:25:18.582-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve starting on port 8787
2026-05-21T21:25:20.583-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting
2026-05-21T21:25:20.584-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s
2026-05-21T21:25:22.266-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T21:25:22.309-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T21:25:22.311-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 37ms
```

**Line-by-line analysis of the fix's behavior:**

1. `[gmail-watcher] watch started for test@gmail.com`: `startGmailWatch` registered with Gmail API (mock gog exits 0).
2. `[gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve ...`: `spawnGogServe` spawned the mock gog process.
3. `[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher started for test@gmail.com (renew every 720m)`: Watcher fully initialized, `renewInterval` set.
4. `[gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting`: Mock gog exits after 2 seconds.
5. **`[gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s`**: The exit handler fires. **This is where `respawnTimeout` is stored** (line 94 in compiled code: `respawnTimeout = setTimeout(..., 5000)`). Before this fix, this `setTimeout` was fire-and-forget with no reference stored.
6. `[gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve ...`: **The respawn timer fired**, spawning a new process. Proves `respawnTimeout` storage works.
7. Second exit + second `restarting in 5s`: Cycle repeats.
8. `[gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down`: SIGTERM arrives during the 5-second respawn window.
9. **`[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped`**: `stopGmailWatcher` ran, which sets `shuttingDown = true` and calls `clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)` (lines 238-240 in compiled code). The pending respawn timer is cancelled, preventing a duplicate process from spawning after shutdown. Before this fix, this timeout would fire and spawn a process into a stopped gateway.

**What this proves:**
- The `respawnTimeout` variable correctly stores the `setTimeout` return value in the exit handler.
- The respawn mechanism works: process exits, timer fires, new process spawns.
- `stopGmailWatcher` correctly clears the pending respawn timer via `clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)`.
- SIGTERM during a respawn window does not leak a duplicate process.

**Vitest test suite (7 tests, supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
 ✓ |hooks| ../../src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts (7 tests) 127ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)
```

The 7 tests cover all re-entry scenarios:

1. **Kills existing process on re-entry**: Verifies `.kill("SIGTERM")` on the old child.
2. **Clears interval on re-entry**: Verifies timer count stays stable.
3. **Only one renewal per tick**: One renewal after double start.
4. **Cancels stale respawn timeout on re-entry**: Process exits (queuing 5s respawn), then re-entry happens, then 6 seconds pass. Verifies exactly 2 spawns (no duplicate from stale timer).
5. **Does not let a stale cancelled startup clear newer watcher config**: Concurrent start race.
6. **Starts gmail watcher**: Happy path.
7. **Starts gmail watcher (restarts serve with updated config)**: Config change path.

Observed result after fix: The mock gog lifecycle proof shows the full respawn cycle working in production compiled code: process spawn, process exit, `respawnTimeout` stored, respawn fires, second cycle, SIGTERM during respawn window, `stopGmailWatcher` clears the pending timeout and shuts down cleanly. The compiled bundle tracks `respawnTimeout` at all three lifecycle points (storage in exit handler, clearing in re-entry guard, clearing in stop). All 7 tests pass.

What was not tested: Triggering the re-entry guard specifically (calling `startGmailWatcher` twice during a live session). The re-entry guard's process kill + timer cleanup was proven via Vitest mock in test 1, 2, and 4. The mock gog proof covers the respawn and stop paths.

target.title: Expose plugin approval action metadata
target.body:
Summary:
- Add plugin approval action templates to the before_tool_call approval result and plugin.approval.request schema.
- Expand templates with the server-generated approval id, store the resulting action commands, and carry them through reply payloads and approval view models.
- Add keepPendingWithoutRoute so external HITL plugins can keep plugin approvals pending when no core approval route/client is active.
- Keep native decision-button channel adapters compatible with command-only approval actions.
- Update the generated Swift protocol model and plugin SDK API baseline for the intentional additive surface change.

Verification:

- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`

## Real behavior proof

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, rebased onto current `origin/main` on May 16, 2026.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the protocol generator/check, plugin SDK API baseline check, extension typecheck, plugin SDK strict declaration smoke, targeted extension oxlint, focused approval/gateway/plugin Vitest coverage, the host hook contract test, and whitespace diff check.

Evidence after fix:
```text
$ pnpm protocol:check
```

Observed result after fix: the protocol and SDK baselines reflect the new additive action metadata, representative adapters preserve command actions, approval view/reply rendering carries those commands, and the before_tool_call host hook contract remains compatible.

What was not tested: full release suite, live World ID callback flow, and the follow-up verified plugin-owned resolution API that is split into #82434.

Refs #82336


## External plugin integration proof

Behavior addressed: the external ClawHub AgentKit plugin can consume the combined temporary OpenClaw host API branch that includes this PR's approval actions and no-route pending behavior.

Real environment tested: GitHub Actions in `Guardiola31337/openclaw-agentkit` on May 16, 2026, checking out `Guardiola31337/openclaw@agentkit/external-plugin-host-apis` and linking it into the external plugin package.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: the remote workflow checked out the temporary OpenClaw host branch, installed with frozen lockfiles, ran `pnpm build gatewayWatch`, ran `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke`, linked the host checkout into the AgentKit plugin, then ran `pnpm test:hitl` and `pnpm pack:check`.

target.title: Fix streamed chat completions dropping leading less-than
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve cumulative assistant `text` payloads when translating agent events into OpenAI-compatible SSE deltas, so leading `<` characters are not dropped when `delta` starts after a buffered tag prefix.
- Defer chat-completion stream finalization by one microtask after lifecycle end/result resolution so same-turn assistant deltas are flushed before `[DONE]`.
- Add regression coverage for split literal XML openers and chat-completions SSE final-fragment flushing.

Fixes #81991

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `/v1/chat/completions` streaming responses now keep a literal leading `<` in assistant content and flush the last assistant text fragment before the OpenAI-compatible SSE `[DONE]` frame.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with Node `v23.11.0` and pnpm `11.1.0`, rebased on current `origin/main` (`3156d94bca`), exercising the repository's OpenAI-compatible HTTP SSE e2e harness for both `gateway-server` and `gateway-client` projects plus the embedded subscribe stream filter path.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm exec node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/gateway/openai-http.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.filters-final-suppresses-output-without-start-tag.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output captured after the refreshed patch:

  ```text
  RUN  v4.1.6 /private/tmp/openclaw-sse-refresh

  Test Files  4 passed (4)
       Tests  70 passed (70)
    Duration  52.38s (transform 25.88s, setup 1.41s, import 16.87s, tests 33.71s, environment 0ms)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The OpenAI-compatible HTTP SSE e2e path emitted the full streamed assistant content in both gateway modes, including the leading `<` case and the final `finish` fragment before stream completion; the embedded subscribe path preserved `<xiaohai-banli>...` when the literal tag opener was split after a bare `<`.
- **What was not tested:** A live external OpenAI/Codex provider call was not used; the proof exercises OpenClaw's local HTTP SSE gateway behavior and stream filtering path without sending provider credentials.

## Tests
- `git diff --cached --check && git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/openai-http.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.filters-final-suppresses-output-without-start-tag.test.ts --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm exec node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/gateway/openai-http.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.filters-final-suppresses-output-without-start-tag.test.ts --reporter=dot` — 4 files / 70 tests passed

target.title: Add plugin manifest contract for SecretRef provider integrations
target.body:
## Summary
- add a manifest-level SecretRef provider integration contract for plugins
- let plugin manifests declare exec provider presets that materialize existing `secrets.providers` config
- expose SDK types and cover manifest normalization/materialization with focused tests

## Why
External providers like Vault, OneCLI, 1Password, Bitwarden, and similar stores were already possible through manual exec SecretRef configuration. This makes them discoverable, portable, and installable as plugins without hardcoding provider-specific logic in OpenClaw core.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: External SecretRef providers could already be wired manually with `exec`, but plugin-owned Vault-style integrations were not discoverable or portable through plugin manifest metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local Podman setup with `quay.io/sallyom/openclaw:latest`, `hashicorp/vault:latest` in dev mode, external `claw-vault` plugin from https://github.com/sallyom/claw-vault, and OpenRouter model `openrouter/openai/gpt-4.1-nano`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started Vault dev mode, seeded `secret/providers/openrouter` from `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, started OpenClaw with `models.providers.openrouter.apiKey` set to `{ "source": "exec", "provider": "vault", "id": "providers/openrouter/apiKey" }`
- Additional live proof on the running openclaw: confirmed the git-installed `claw-vault` manifest declares `secretProviderIntegrations.vault` with `command: "${node}"` and `args: ["./vault-secret-ref-resolver.js"]`, confirmed model API keys are SecretRefs using provider `vault`, ran `openclaw secrets audit --allow-exec --json`, then ran `openclaw agent --local --agent somalley_larry --message "Reply with exactly: vault-secretref-larry-ok" --timeout 60000 --json`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Full terminal-command repro is in this Gist: https://gist.github.com/sallyom/50f4678b053eaa93c4311a5509e52aeb. Copied live output from `secrets reload`: `{"ok":true,"warningCount":0}`. Copied live output from the agent run included `vault-secretref-ok`. 
- Observed result after fix: OpenClaw reloaded SecretRefs with zero warnings in the original repro and completed live model turns using API keys resolved from Vault by the external `claw-vault` exec resolver. Also resolved two Vault-backed exec SecretRefs with no unresolved refs and completed a live OpenRouter agent turn through the Vault-resolved key.
- Users would use the external Vault plugin's `openclaw vault setup` helper to generate an OpenClaw secrets apply plan, for example `openclaw vault setup --openrouter-id providers/openrouter/apiKey --plan-out ./vault-secrets-plan.json`, then apply it with the generic secrets command. `openclaw vault setup` maps existing Vault secret ids into OpenClaw SecretRefs and provider config; it does not write secret values into Vault. `openclaw vault status --json` reports whether the Vault provider is configured.
- What was not tested: Production Vault deployment, non-dev Vault auth methods, 1Password/Bitwarden/OneCLI plugins.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this contract, the same Vault resolver could only be wired by hand through explicit `secrets.providers.vault` config; there was no plugin manifest preset for OpenClaw to discover/materialize.

## Verification
- `pnpm vitest run src/secrets/provider-integrations.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

target.title: feat(telegram): interleave CLI tool-progress + reasoning + rolling timer in one Telegram message
target.body:
> **Rescope (2026-05-20):** This PR previously also added a parallel CLI tool-event emitter in `cli-output.ts` and a followup-path bridge in `agent-runner-execution.ts`. Those overlap with [#80046](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80046) which adds the canonical CLI tool-event surface (`dispatchClaudeCliStreamingToolEvent` + `onToolUseStart` / `onToolResult` parser callbacks, three claude `stream-json` record shapes, args+result sanitisation, isCliProvider-gated bridge). Landing both would have shipped duplicate emitters with divergent contracts. Commit `70496edf7c` reverts those overlapping changes; #82285 now reduces to the **Telegram-side rendering layer** that consumes the agent-event bus #80046 publishes. Depends on #80046 landing first.

---

## Summary

- **Problem.** When Claude calls a tool mid-turn on the `claude-cli` backend, the assistant text stream just pauses — often for tens of seconds on `Read` / `Bash` / `Grep` / `WebFetch`. The end user sees a frozen typing indicator and no signal about which tool is running.
- **Why it matters.** Looks like the gateway hung; users abandon turns that are still in flight. Tool calls live in `stream_event` records, never in `assistant_text` deltas, so the existing assistant-stream consumer never surfaces them.
- **What changed.** Two complementary surfaces on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` (gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`):
  1. **In-stream textual marker** via `onAssistantDelta` — `\n\n[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail\n` followed by a rolling `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator that refreshes every 8s, stripped on the next `text_delta` or on turn end.
  2. **Structured `stream: "tool"` agent event** (`{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}`) via a new optional `onToolEvent` callback — consumed by `cli-runner/execute.ts` and forwarded to the agent event bus for downstream tool-tile UIs.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect({backend, providerId})` — no impact on non-Claude CLI backends. Existing `onAssistantDelta` callers are unaffected. `onToolEvent` is opt-in. No tool-output / tool-result event in this PR (the result is reflected in claude's next `text_delta` and the timer-strip handles UX). Tool args sanitized via `sanitizeToolArgs` before either path.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** tool calls during a Claude turn now produce both an in-stream textual marker (with rolling 8s timer indicator) and a structured `stream: "tool"` agent event, when `backend.jsonlDialect === "claude-stream-json"` or `providerId === "claude-cli"`.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142, Opus 4.7, live OpenClaw gateway driving the `claude-cli` backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`; live Telegram turn that invokes one or more tools (Read / Bash / Grep / WebFetch); observe the textual marker, the 8s tick refresh, and the marker stripping plus `\n\n` separator when assistant text resumes; verify the `stream: "tool"` agent event in `emitAgentEvent` logs.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - Telegram screenshot of a live turn (rolling-timer rendered to the end-user surface): ![telegram rolling-timer](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-82285/.proof/telegram-rolling-timer.jpg). Every `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Bash: …` line in the screenshot is one `emitTool` firing in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` against `claude-cli`.
  - Typecheck clean: `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`.
  - The markers are an in-flight overlay delivered via `onAssistantDelta`, not persisted to `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl` (which stores claude's structured `result`) or `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` at default verbosity — Telegram is the canonical capture surface.
- **Observed result after fix:** tool calls render as `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail` blocks in the assistant text, with the live timer ticking every 8s. When claude resumes streaming text, the timer is stripped and the text continues from a clean `\n\n` boundary. Agent-event consumers receive a structured `{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}` for each tool call.
- **What was not tested:** end-to-end against a dashboard / external listener for the `stream: "tool"` event surface (event is emitted, visible in `emitAgentEvent` logs, but no downstream UI verified beyond that); Linux/macOS execution (developed on Windows only); bundled MCP tool paths (`server_tool_use` / `mcp_tool_use` content-block types are matched in the tracker but I have not driven a live MCP-tool turn through it); focused unit test for `createClaudeToolUseTracker` is not added yet (see Regression Test Plan).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Adds opt-in `onToolEvent` callback on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` + in-stream textual marker over the existing `onAssistantDelta`.
- Overlap to coordinate: #80046 (CLI tool-event bridge). Maintainers' judgment on whether inline rolling markers ride alongside or after the structured bridge.

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The condition this addresses (no tool-call UX during turns) is a property of the Claude CLI JSONL surface (tool calls live in `stream_event`, never in `assistant_text` deltas), not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` — add a `describe("tool-use tracker", …)` block.
- **Scenario locked in:** simulated `stream_event` sequence — `message_start` → `content_block_start (tool_use, name: "Read")` → 3× `content_block_delta (input_json_delta partial)` → `content_block_stop` — asserts the resulting `ClaudeToolEvent` carries the reassembled `args.file_path`, the right `itemId`, and that the parser's `assistantText` now includes the `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Read: <path>` line.
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode (regression of partial-JSON accumulation, wrong `content_block_stop` dispatch, verbose-gating drift) is fully exercisable by calling the parser's `push()` with a canned JSONL transcript — no live claude needed. Runs in ms.
- **Honest gap:** I have **not** added this test yet. Behaviour is exercised live in my local environment but a frozen-transcript unit test belongs with this PR before flipping out of draft. Flagging as a must-add before un-drafting.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** partial-JSON accumulation in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` could drop or corrupt tool args if `content_block_stop` fires before all `input_json_delta` chunks arrive.
  - **Mitigation:** tracker holds the entry until `content_block_stop`, then parses `entry.input ?? entry.partialJson` via `parseClaudeToolArgs`. The pending unit test (`cli-output.test.ts`) locks in the contract for partial-JSON reassembly.
- **Risk:** rolling-timer `setInterval` leaks if a `content_block_stop` arrives but no subsequent `text_delta` fires (e.g. turn aborts mid-tool).
  - **Mitigation:** `clearToolKeepalive()` is also called on `result`, on `finish()`, and on the next `text_delta`. The parser closure owns the interval handle and explicitly clears it on every terminal path.
- **Risk:** tool args contain sensitive content (file paths, credentials in `WebFetch` URLs, etc.) and surface in both Telegram and agent events.
  - **Mitigation:** `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates and redacts known-sensitive keys before either emission path. Verbose gating (`shouldInjectToolInlineMarkers`) lets sessions opt out of inline markers without losing the structured event.
- **Risk:** the inline marker rides on `onAssistantDelta` — any consumer that strict-parses assistant text as Markdown could choke on the `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator.
  - **Mitigation:** marker uses a deliberately conservative `\n\n[…] 🛠️ …\n` shape that's Markdown-safe (Telegram renders it verbatim). Consumers wanting strict text can ignore by subscribing only to the structured `stream: "tool"` event.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — same allowed-tools set; `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates args before emission.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same tools claude already executes; this PR only renders that they're in flight.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — tracker reads `stream_event` records the parser already processes.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`; non-Claude backends unaffected.
- `onToolEvent` is an additive optional callback — existing parser callers don't need to wire it up.
- No new config, no new env vars. Disable by not subscribing to the new event surface.

target.title: feat(slack): render progress as native progress task cards
target.body:
## Summary
- Add Slack native progress task cards as an explicit opt-in feature for `channels.slack.streaming.mode="progress"` via `channels.slack.streaming.progress.nativeTaskCards=true`.
- Preserve existing progress-mode behavior by keeping the portable draft-preview path when the new opt-in is unset.
- Keep Slack native progress plan/task rendering and completion updates for opted-in accounts, including first running-tool visibility before final text through Slack `plan_update` / `task_update` stream chunks.
- Keep `nativeTaskCards` scoped to Slack config while preserving the public `SlackChannelStreamingConfig` type export and stable progress identity fields used by the Slack path.

## Maintainer review guide
- Default behavior should remain unchanged: Slack `mode="progress"` still uses the portable draft-preview path unless `channels.slack.streaming.progress.nativeTaskCards=true` is set.
- Config/API boundary: `nativeTaskCards` is Slack-only config (`src/config/types.slack.ts`, `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts`) and is not part of the shared channel progress schema.
- Runtime boundary: `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts` gates the opted-in path, starts/appends/stops the Slack native stream, and keeps final replies in the same thread.
- Rendering boundary: `extensions/slack/src/progress-blocks.ts` converts progress lines into Slack `plan_update` / `task_update` chunks and keeps stable task ids when upstream events provide item/tool ids.
- Slack transport boundary: `extensions/slack/src/streaming.ts` extends the existing Slack stream helper to carry structured chunks alongside text.
- Best review path: check the opt-in gate, the chunk construction, the stream lifecycle/finalization path, then the focused dispatch/renderer tests listed below.

## Suggested follow-ups
- Stable identity for repeated same-name tools when upstream progress events do not provide item/tool ids. This PR preserves ids when present, but could be broadened in a separate follow-up if maintainers want stricter repeated-tool identity.
- More precise native task finalization for failed commands followed by a normal assistant reply. This PR finalizes visible in-progress native tasks for the opt-in path, but deeper command-outcome propagation could be handled in a separate follow-up if maintainers want that behavior tightened.
